Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida.
== See Also == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida.
== See Also == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}

== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida.
== See Also == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}

revision.text:
[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida.
== See Also == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}

revision.text:
[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida.
== See Also == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida.== See Also == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>
== Yuta in Popular Culture == === Television === * *{{ill|Ultraseven|ja|ウルトラセブン}}* – Episode 34 "Vaporized City" (1968). * *{{ill|FE Far East|ja|FE ファーイースト 序章}}* (2013). * *{{ill|Segodon (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|西郷どん (NHK大河ドラマ)}}* (2018).
=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
=== Novels === * *{{ill|Bagaa Jimanu Panasu|ja|バガージマヌパナス}}* by Eichi Ikegami. * *{{ill|Yuta ga Aishita Tantei|ja|ユタが愛した探偵}}* by Yasuo Uchida. == See Also! == * {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} * {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} * {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Shinto|ja|琉球神道}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[File:Yuta circa 1955.JPG|thumb|250px|A Yuta performing a ritual (circa 1955)]] '''Yuta''' (ユタ) are folk mediums ([[Shamanism|shamans]]) in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}}. They are spiritual advisors and problem solvers, providing guidance on spiritual and everyday matters.
== Overview == According to {{ill|Tokutaro Sakurai|ja|桜井徳太郎}}, the most prominent [[Shamanism|shamanic]] activities in the Japanese archipelago are found in {{ill|Tohoku region|ja|東北地方}} and the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|南西諸島}}. Shamans who enter a trance by channeling the spirits of the dead and relay messages in the first person are generally referred to as {{ill|Kuchiyose miko|ja|口寄せ巫女}}. Similar to the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}} of Tohoku, the folk shamans of the Nansei Islands are known as '''Yuta''', '''Hozon''', and '''Toki''' (in the Amami and Okinawa Islands), '''Kankakarya''' and '''Sasu''' (in the Miyako Islands), and '''Munuchi''', '''Nigeebii''', and '''Kanpitu''' (in the Yaeyama Islands).<ref name="kenkyuge7-0">Sakurai Tokutaro, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6: Research on Japanese Shamanism (Part 2) - Structure and Function*, 1988, Chapter 7.</ref>
In the folk society of the Ryukyu Islands, the religious roles are divided between female priestesses, such as {{ill|Noro|ja|ノロ}} (high priestesses), {{ill|Neigan|ja|根神}}, and {{ill|Sasu|ja|サス}}, collectively called {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|Kaminchu=Kaminchu|lt=Kaminchu}} (sacred persons), and shamans like the Yuta. The former officiate public rituals in sacred places like {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)}} and {{ill|Gusuku|ja|グスク}}, leading communal prayers. In contrast, Yuta engage in private spiritual matters, such as divining fortunes (''unchi''), determining auspicious and inauspicious omens (''hanji''), exorcisms (''harii''), and healing prayers (''ugwan''). Unlike priestesses, who avoid death-related rituals, Yuta specialize in funerary rites and ancestral veneration.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-1">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref>
Yuta are believed to possess supernatural abilities that allow them to perceive the spiritual world beyond the reach of ordinary people.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-0">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref> However, among Okinawan educators and intellectuals, Yuta practices are often dismissed as superstitions, and in formal settings, they are rarely acknowledged.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 1.</ref> Despite this, many prominent figures—including bureaucrats, government officials, teachers, and businessmen—have historically consulted Yuta for guidance.<ref name="kenkyuge7-2-2">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 2.</ref>
The practice of seeking guidance from Yuta, known as ''Yuta-kōya'' (ユタ買い), often involves consulting multiple Yuta to confirm their readings. People willingly pay substantial fees for their services.<ref name="kenkyuge7-1-4" /> An old Okinawan saying states, "Half doctor, half Yuta," reflecting the enduring belief in both medical and spiritual healing.
== Becoming a Yuta == Yuta are believed to be chosen by fate. Many experience a phenomenon called ''kamidari'' (カミダーリィ), an intense spiritual awakening often accompanied by visions, trances, or illnesses that medical treatment cannot cure. It is believed that those who resist their calling will suffer misfortune or worsening health.<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-3">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
Those undergoing ''kamidari'' seek guidance from experienced Yuta, who initiate them into the practice through spiritual training and pilgrimage to sacred sites (''ugwan-maai''). Unlike the structured apprenticeship of the {{ill|Itako|ja|イタコ}}, the training of a Yuta is informal, often involving observation and participation in rituals until they gradually acquire recognition as a full-fledged practitioner. This state of independence is called ''michiake'' (道あけ).<ref name="kenkyuge7-3-4">Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7, Section 3.</ref>
== Historical Persecution == Due to their role as independent spiritual leaders, Yuta have been subject to periodic suppression: 1. The Ryukyu Kingdom's prohibition under the administration of {{ill|Sai On|ja|蔡温}}. 2. Local bans during the Meiji period. 3. The anti-Yuta campaign of the Taisho period. 4. Crackdowns during the militarist regime of the 1930s.
These measures often resulted in the arrest or detainment of Yuta by authorities. Despite these challenges, Yuta practices persist to this day.
== Modern Issues == Many fraudulent individuals exploit the reputation of Yuta for financial gain. Some manipulate vulnerable clients by instilling fear, leading to financial exploitation akin to [[:en:Religious_fraud|religious fraud]]. This has contributed to a negative perception of Yuta, and the phrase "''Yuta-kōya-ya, chuu-orasee''" (ユタコーヤーヤ、チュオーラセー)—"Buying a Yuta creates conflict"—warns against over-reliance on their services.
== Classification as a Shaman == Scholars classify Yuta as: - **"Call-type shamans"** (召命型シャーマン): They do not train formally but are "called" through ''kamidari''. - **"Possession-type shamans"** (憑依型シャーマン): Their abilities manifest through spirit possession rather than trance-induced out-of-body experiences.<ref>Sakurai, *Collected Works of Tokutaro Sakurai, Volume 6*, Chapter 7.</ref>

=== Films === * *{{ill|Nabbie’s Love|ja|ナビィの恋}}* (1999). * *{{ill|Akaokuro|ja|アコークロー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|Sadaka|ja|サーダカー}}* (2007). * *{{ill|The Tempest|ja|テンペスト (池上永一)}}* (2011). * *{{ill|It Comes (film)|ja|ぼぎわんが、来る}}* (2018).
